heroOverview: Myelophthisis (ICD-10: D61.82) is a form of bone marrow failure in which normal haematopoietic tissue is displaced by infiltrating abnormal cells or materials — most commonly metastatic cancer, primary haematological malignancies, granulomatous infections, or storage disorders — thereby preventing adequate production of red blood cells, white cells, and platelets. The term derives from the Greek phthisis (wasting away), reflecting the progressive consumption of normal marrow space. It is not a primary disease itself but a pathophysiological syndrome; the underlying disorder causing the marrow infiltration determines prognosis and treatment. The cardinal laboratory hallmark is a leukoerythroblastic blood picture — circulating nucleated red cells, immature granulocytes, and characteristic teardrop-shaped erythrocytes — which reflects both the mechanical displacement of marrow sinusoids and the premature release of haematopoietic progenitors from extramedullary sites including the spleen and liver. Any solid tumour metastasising to bone marrow, breast cancer and prostate cancer most commonly, can cause myelophthisis, making this a key differential in cancer patients presenting with unexplained pancytopenia.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact incidence unknown; bone marrow metastases — the most common cause of myelophthisis — are found in 15-30% of autopsied patients with disseminated solid tumours
keyStats.demographics: Adults predominantly; most common in patients with known disseminated malignancy (breast, prostate, lung, neuroblastoma in children); no sex or ethnic predilection independent of the underlying disease
keyStats.avgAge: Reflects the age distribution of the causative disease; solid tumour-related myelophthisis peaks in adults aged 50-75
keyStats.globalCases: No global registry; estimated to affect hundreds of thousands annually given the frequency of metastatic cancer worldwide

definition: Myelophthisis (ICD-10: D61.82) is a myelopathic anaemia characterised by replacement of the normal bone marrow haematopoietic microenvironment by infiltrating neoplastic, inflammatory, or metabolic tissue, resulting in impaired trilineage blood cell production. The condition is classified under aplastic anaemia and other bone marrow failure syndromes in ICD-10. The pathophysiological hallmark is a leukoerythroblastic reaction on the peripheral blood film: nucleated erythrocytes (normoblasts) and immature granulocytes (myelocytes, metamyelocytes) circulate because disruption of the marrow vascular sinusoids and reduced trabecular integrity permits premature egress of immature cells. Characteristic teardrop-shaped red cells (dacrocytes) form as erythrocytes are deformed while squeezing past marrow fibrosis or tumour deposits. In addition to space occupation, infiltrating cells release cytokines including TGF-β, IL-6, and CXCL12 that suppress residual normal haematopoietic stem cell function, creating both mechanical and functional marrow failure. The most common causes in adults are metastatic carcinoma (particularly breast cancer and prostate cancer), primary haematological malignancies (lymphoma, leukaemia, multiple myeloma), marrow fibrosis (osteomyelofibrosis), granulomatous infections (tuberculosis, histoplasmosis), and lipid storage disorders (Gaucher disease). In children, neuroblastoma and other small round cell tumours that disseminate to marrow are leading causes. Falls under haematology and is managed in conjunction with the specialist treating the underlying infiltrating disease.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause in adults; breast cancer, prostate cancer, lung cancer, and gastric cancer most frequently metastasise to the bone marrow, replacing haematopoietic tissue with tumour deposits and reactive fibrosis.

Lymphoma, multiple myeloma, leukaemia, and Waldenstrom macroglobulinaemia can infiltrate the marrow diffusely, displacing normal cells; myeloma in particular causes marrow failure through plasmacytoma infiltration.

Progressive replacement of marrow by fibrous connective tissue (osteomyelofibrosis, D47.4) prevents normal haematopoiesis through a mechanistic overlap; this is the prototypical non-malignant infiltrative cause.

Disseminated tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, brucellosis, and sarcoidosis can extensively infiltrate the marrow with granulomata, causing myelophthisic anaemia that resolves when the infection is treated.

Gaucher disease (glucocerebrosidase deficiency) fills marrow macrophages with glucocerebroside, compressing haematopoietic space; Niemann-Pick and other lysosomal storage disorders occasionally cause the same pattern.

primarySymptoms: Progressive anaemia manifesting as fatigue, pallor, exertional dyspnoea, and palpitations — usually normocytic and normochromic, reflecting combined impaired erythroid production and premature cell release from disrupted marrow.
Easy bruising and spontaneous bleeding from thrombocytopenia — petechiae, gum bleeding, prolonged oozing after minor cuts — as megakaryocyte production is impaired by progressive marrow replacement.

Recurrent or unusually severe bacterial infections related to functional neutropenia, even when total white count may be normal or elevated, because the circulating cells are disproportionately immature and dysfunctional.

Bone pain — often diffuse, involving the axial skeleton (spine, pelvis, ribs) and proximal long bones — reflecting periosteal stretching by infiltrating tumour or marrow expansion by lipid-laden macrophages in storage disorders.

Symptoms of the underlying systemic disease driving marrow infiltration — in metastatic cancer: weight loss, lymphadenopathy, and organ-specific symptoms; in tuberculosis: fever, night sweats, productive cough; in Gaucher disease: abdominal distension from organomegaly.

Splenomegaly and hepatomegaly due to extramedullary haematopoiesis as the spleen and liver attempt to compensate for reduced marrow output, contributing to abdominal fullness and left upper quadrant discomfort.
Jaundice or elevated serum LDH reflecting haemolysis of prematurely released and fragile erythrocytes and rapid tumour cell turnover respectively.

Pathological fractures — vertebral compression fractures or long bone fractures through metastatic deposits — presenting as acute localised bone pain, loss of height, or neurological deficits from cord compression.
Fevers and night sweats without identified infection in patients with lymphoma or myeloma infiltrating the marrow, reflecting inflammatory cytokine excess.
Constitutional wasting — muscle loss, anorexia, and cachexia — in advanced cancer-related myelophthisis, compounding the haematological failure with a deteriorating performance status.
earlyWarningSigns: A new leukoerythroblastic picture on the blood film — teardrop cells, nucleated red cells, and a left shift to myelocytes — in any patient with known disseminated malignancy or systemic inflammatory disease.
An unexplained fall in haemoglobin, platelet count, or white cell count over weeks in a cancer patient previously maintaining stable blood counts.
Bone pain out of proportion to any identifiable skeletal injury in a patient with a known malignancy, which should prompt bone imaging and consideration of a bone marrow biopsy.

A raised serum LDH or alkaline phosphatase with progressive normocytic anaemia in a patient with prior cancer or a systemic febrile illness — these biochemical signals suggest marrow infiltration or extensive bone metastases.
emergencySigns: Spinal cord compression — back pain with leg weakness, sensory loss, or loss of bladder and bowel control in a cancer patient — is an oncological emergency requiring immediate imaging (MRI spine) and treatment to prevent permanent paralysis.
Severe haemorrhage from profound thrombocytopenia (platelets <10×10⁹/L) — intracranial, gastrointestinal, or pulmonary bleeding — requiring emergency platelet transfusion.

Neutropenic fever (temperature >38.3°C with absolute neutrophil count <500/µL) presenting with signs of sepsis — hypotension, confusion, rigors — which requires immediate empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics.

Acute hypercalcaemia (corrected calcium >3.0 mmol/L) — confusion, severe dehydration, cardiac arrhythmia — from osteolytic bone metastases causing massive calcium release, requiring immediate IV hydration and bisphosphonate therapy.

causes.description: Breast cancer, prostate cancer, lung cancer, thyroid cancer, renal cell carcinoma, and neuroblastoma (in children) are the most common solid tumours that metastasise to bone marrow; disseminated tumour cells occupy haematopoietic space, induce reactive fibrosis via TGF-β secretion, and suppress normal progenitor cell growth through cytokine and direct cell-contact mechanisms.

Multiple myeloma, lymphoma (both Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin), chronic leukaemia, and Waldenstrom macroglobulinaemia infiltrate the marrow with neoplastic lymphoid or plasma cells that directly displace haematopoietic tissue and impair normal stem cell function through paracrine cytokine suppression.

Progressive replacement of marrow with reticulin and collagen fibrosis driven by clonal megakaryocyte-derived TGF-β represents a mechanistically distinct but clinically similar cause of myelophthisis; the marrow space is occupied by fibrous tissue rather than infiltrating cells, but the haematological consequences are identical.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Mycobacterium avium complex, Histoplasma capsulatum, Brucella species, and Leishmania donovani disseminate to the bone marrow in immunocompromised or severely immunocompromised patients, forming granulomata that replace normal haematopoietic cells and produce myelophthisis that reverses with effective antimicrobial treatment.

causes.cause: Gaucher disease and lipid storage disorders
causes.description: In Gaucher disease (autosomal recessive glucocerebrosidase deficiency), lipid-laden macrophages (Gaucher cells) infiltrate the marrow, spleen, and liver over years to decades; the resulting marrow failure causes anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and bone crises, and is specifically treated with enzyme replacement therapy with imiglucerase or velaglucerase alfa.

Bone marrow involvement in sarcoidosis — found in up to 30% of systemic sarcoidosis patients on bone marrow biopsy — produces granulomata that occupy marrow space and can cause mild to moderate cytopenias; the myelophthisis resolves with corticosteroid treatment of the underlying sarcoidosis.

riskFactors.description: Patients with stage IV breast, prostate, lung, or renal cancer have the highest risk of haematogenous bone marrow metastasis; prevalence of bone marrow involvement rises with increasing tumour burden and haematogenous spread.

Bone marrow is the primary site of disease in myeloma and is commonly infiltrated in lymphoma; marrow plasma cell percentage above 60% in myeloma is directly associated with severe cytopenias from myelophthisis.

Immunosuppressed patients are at risk of disseminated opportunistic infections — tuberculosis, histoplasmosis, Leishmania — that infect bone marrow and produce myelophthisis; CD4+ counts below 100 cells/µL substantially raise this risk.

Gaucher disease type 1 is the most common lysosomal storage disorder associated with bone marrow infiltration; biallelic GBA loss-of-function mutations are required; Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry confers substantially higher carrier frequency (1 in 10 individuals).

Tumours that have progressed through multiple lines of treatment have higher metastatic burden including marrow involvement; patients with hormone-refractory prostate cancer or triple-negative breast cancer are at particular risk.

In TB-endemic regions (sub-Saharan Africa, South/Southeast Asia), disseminated tuberculosis remains a common cause of myelophthisis particularly in HIV-coinfected individuals; diagnosis is frequently delayed because the haematological presentation overshadows the infectious symptoms.

affectedDemographics: Adults with advanced metastatic solid tumours, particularly breast and prostate cancer
Patients with multiple myeloma or high-grade lymphoma with extensive marrow involvement
Immunocompromised patients with disseminated opportunistic infections (tuberculosis, histoplasmosis)
Children with disseminated neuroblastoma or Ewing sarcoma metastasising to marrow
Adults and children with Gaucher disease type 1 with longstanding enzyme deficiency
Patients in TB-endemic regions with advanced or disseminated tuberculosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of myelophthisis begins with recognition of the leukoerythroblastic blood picture on peripheral film — the most reliable clinical sign that prompts bone marrow evaluation. This pattern, combined with a history of known malignancy, systemic infection, or storage disorder, points toward marrow infiltration. Bone marrow trephine biopsy is the definitive diagnostic procedure: it identifies the infiltrating cell type (metastatic carcinoma, lymphoma cells, granulomata, Gaucher cells, or fibrosis), quantifies the degree of haematopoietic replacement, and provides tissue for immunohistochemistry, special stains (for organisms, lipid), flow cytometry, and molecular testing. The aspiration is frequently a dry tap due to marrow fibrosis or tight packing. Bilateral posterior iliac crest biopsies increase diagnostic yield by approximately 20% compared with unilateral biopsy for patchy infiltrations. Imaging guides the clinical picture: bone marrow MRI reveals diffuse low T1 signal replacement in extensive infiltration; PET-CT localises metabolically active disease. In patients without a prior cancer diagnosis, the causative primary tumour must be identified through directed biopsy and imaging. For suspected infectious aetiology, blood cultures, mycobacterial cultures (including bone marrow culture — yield approximately 40-60% in disseminated TB), fungal cultures, and serologies are essential. Serum protein electrophoresis and free light chain assay detect myeloma. LDH, beta-2 microglobulin, ferritin, and uric acid add prognostic information. Genetic testing for GBA mutations establishes a Gaucher disease diagnosis and is confirmed by leukocyte glucocerebrosidase activity assay (gold standard: activity <10-15% of normal).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the leukoerythroblastic pattern — teardrop cells (dacrocytes), nucleated red cells, and immature granulocytes — which is the cardinal indicator of marrow infiltration and the key trigger for bone marrow biopsy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; a thin smear is examined microscopically by a haematopathologist; results in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow trephine biopsy (bilateral if needed)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive test — provides a cylindrical marrow core for histological assessment of the infiltrating material type, extent of haematopoietic replacement, and tissue for immunohistochemistry and special stains.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthetic at the posterior iliac crest; a trephine needle withdraws a core of bone and marrow; brief deep pressure pain during the procedure; results in 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow aspirate with flow cytometry and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies haematological malignancy cell surface markers and obtains material for microbiological culture (mycobacterial, fungal) when infection is suspected; often yields a dry tap in fibrotic or tightly packed marrow.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed at the same time as the trephine biopsy using the same access site; additional sample collected for flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and cultures.

diagnosticTests.test: Whole-body bone marrow MRI or PET-CT
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive evaluation of the extent of marrow infiltration; MRI shows diffuse replacement of normal fatty marrow signal by tumour or fibrosis; PET-CT identifies metabolically active disease throughout the skeleton and guides biopsy targeting.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30-60 minutes; PET-CT requires a radiotracer injection 60 minutes before scanning; neither is painful.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum protein electrophoresis and free light chains
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects monoclonal paraproteins indicating multiple myeloma or Waldenstrom macroglobulinaemia as the cause of marrow infiltration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results in 24-48 hours; sometimes supplemented by urine protein electrophoresis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard enzymatic diagnosis of Gaucher disease; a result below 10-15% of mean normal activity confirms the diagnosis and eliminates the need for marrow biopsy in most cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn into a specific tube; sent to a specialist biochemistry laboratory; results in 5-14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Mycobacterial and fungal blood and bone marrow cultures
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies disseminated tuberculosis, MAC, histoplasmosis, or other granulomatous pathogens causing infectious myelophthisis; bone marrow culture has approximately 40-60% yield in disseminated tuberculosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn in specific culture bottles; bone marrow aspirate sample collected at biopsy; culture results may take 2-6 weeks for mycobacteria.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of myelophthisis is directed entirely at the underlying infiltrating disease; there is no specific treatment for the syndrome itself. Haematological support maintains safe blood counts while the causative disease is addressed. For metastatic carcinoma — the most common cause — systemic anti-cancer therapy (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, hormonal therapy for breast and prostate cancer, immunotherapy) is directed at the primary disease; responses that reduce marrow tumour burden predictably improve blood counts. Bone-targeted agents such as bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) and denosumab reduce skeletal-related events and may reduce marrow tumour load when osteolytic disease is prominent. For myeloma, plasma cell-directed therapy with bortezomib-based regimens, lenalidomide-based combinations, or daratumumab reduces marrow plasma cell percentage and restores haematopoiesis; a proportion of eligible patients proceed to autologous stem cell transplantation. For lymphoma, lymphodepleting chemotherapy followed by anti-CD20 agents or targeted therapy reduces marrow burden. Granulomatous infectious myelophthisis — including tuberculosis and histoplasmosis — often resolves haematologically with adequate antimicrobial treatment over weeks to months, making rapid pathogen identification critical. Gaucher disease is treated with enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) using imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa, or taliglucerase alfa, which reduces marrow macrophage lipid burden and reverses cytopenias over months to years; substrate reduction therapy (eliglustat, miglustat) is an oral alternative for eligible patients. Haematological support includes red cell transfusion for symptomatic anaemia (target haemoglobin guided by symptoms and comorbidities), platelet transfusion when counts fall below 10-20×10⁹/L or before procedures, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) to reduce infection risk when neutropenia is severe, and erythropoiesis-stimulating agents in selected patients. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation addresses the underlying disease in selected haematological malignancies.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-cancer systemic therapy (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Treatment of the underlying metastatic solid tumour or haematological malignancy to reduce marrow tumour burden; choice of regimen depends on tumour type, molecular target, and prior therapy. Response in marrow typically lags systemic response by 4-8 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Marrow response parallels systemic response; haematological improvement observed in approximately 30-60% of patients who achieve a partial or complete systemic remission; rates vary widely by tumour type and treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Enzyme replacement therapy for Gaucher disease (imiglucerase, velaglucerase alfa)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous recombinant glucocerebrosidase enzyme given every 2 weeks reduces lysosomal glucocerebroside accumulation in marrow macrophages, progressively restoring haematopoietic space and improving blood counts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin improvement of ≥1 g/dL in approximately 60-80% of anaemic Gaucher patients within 12 months; platelet count improvement in approximately 70% within 24 months of adequate dosing; considered standard of care.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-myeloma therapy (bortezomib-based or daratumumab-based regimens)
medicalTreatments.description: Proteasome inhibitor-based and CD38-targeted combinations reduce marrow plasma cell percentage in multiple myeloma, the direct cause of myelophthisic marrow failure; partial responses of ≥50% reduction in plasma cell burden restore haematopoiesis in many patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Marrow complete response (plasma cell percentage <5%) achieved in approximately 20-40% of patients with first-line bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone (VRd); associated with significant improvement in all blood count lineages.

medicalTreatments.name: Antimicrobial therapy for infectious myelophthisis
medicalTreatments.description: Standard anti-tuberculosis therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 6 months) or antifungal therapy (liposomal amphotericin B or itraconazole for histoplasmosis) directly eliminates the granulomatous marrow infiltration; haematological recovery begins within 4-8 weeks of effective treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haematological improvement in approximately 60-80% of patients with disseminated TB myelophthisis who complete standard 6-month therapy; faster recovery observed when diagnosis and treatment are initiated before severe pancytopenia develops.

medicalTreatments.name: Bisphosphonate therapy (zoledronic acid) for bone metastases
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous zoledronic acid given monthly reduces skeletal-related events including pathological fractures, hypercalcaemia, and spinal cord compression in patients with bone metastases; it may have a modest anti-tumour effect on marrow deposits in some cancers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces skeletal-related events by approximately 30-40% versus placebo in breast and prostate cancer bone metastasis trials; indirect benefit to marrow function through reduced osteolytic metastatic burden.

medicalTreatments.name: Red cell and platelet transfusion support
medicalTreatments.description: Packed red cell transfusions maintain symptomatic haemoglobin and quality of life; platelet transfusions prevent life-threatening haemorrhage. A restrictive transfusion strategy (target Hb ≥70-80 g/L in non-cardiac patients) reduces iron overload from chronic transfusion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate correction of severe cytopenias; does not treat the underlying cause but sustains patient through definitive therapy; approximately 30% of myelophthisis patients become transfusion-dependent without control of the underlying disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT)
surgicalOptions.description: Potentially curative for selected haematological malignancies causing myelophthisis (lymphoma, leukaemia, myeloma in eligible patients, primary myelofibrosis); not applicable to metastatic solid tumours. HSCT replaces the entire haematopoietic compartment, resolving marrow failure along with the malignant infiltrate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year overall survival 40-60% for haematological malignancies in appropriate candidates; significantly lower in blast-phase disease or heavily pre-treated patients; non-relapse mortality 15-25% in most series.

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal decompression or orthopaedic fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical decompression is performed urgently when pathological fracture or metastatic vertebral disease causes spinal cord compression; prophylactic intramedullary nailing is considered for long bones with impending fracture (>50% cortical involvement on imaging).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological preservation or improvement in approximately 60-70% of spinal decompression cases performed within 24-48 hours of symptom onset; outcomes deteriorate substantially with delay.

alternativeTreatments.name: Palliative radiotherapy to marrow-bearing bones
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-dose radiation (typically 8-20 Gy in 1-5 fractions) to localised marrow metastases reduces pain and may reduce tumour volume in individual marrow compartments, potentially allowing partial haematopoietic recovery at irradiated sites.

alternativeTreatments.name: Substrate reduction therapy for Gaucher disease (eliglustat, miglustat)
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral agents that reduce glucocerebroside synthesis rather than providing replacement enzyme; eliglustat is preferred for eligible adults with confirmed CYP2D6 metaboliser status; avoids IV infusion but has more drug-drug interactions and absolute contraindications.

whySeeSpecialist: Myelophthisis requires urgent haematology assessment because the leukoerythroblastic blood picture represents a medical alarm demanding rapid identification of the underlying cause. A haematologist is essential for bone marrow biopsy interpretation, risk stratification, and transfusion management, and must coordinate with the oncologist, infectious disease specialist, or metabolic specialist treating the infiltrating disease. Delays in diagnosis — particularly in disseminated infection or newly presenting solid tumours — directly worsen outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: The specialist who manages myelophthisis depends on the underlying cause. A haematologist should be involved in all cases for bone marrow biopsy and blood count support. Oncology co-management is essential when metastatic cancer is the cause. Infectious disease specialists should lead treatment of disseminated TB or fungal myelophthisis. Gaucher disease requires a metabolic or lysosomal storage disorder specialist. Ask whether the centre has expertise in bilateral bone marrow biopsy technique and access to same-day haematopathology review when clinical urgency is high.
preventionStrategies: Adherence to regular oncology follow-up and surveillance imaging in patients with treated malignancies reduces the risk of delayed diagnosis of marrow metastasis.

HIV-positive patients on antiretroviral therapy maintaining CD4+ counts above 200 cells/µL substantially reduce their risk of disseminated opportunistic infections including tuberculosis and histoplasmosis causing myelophthisis.

Enzyme replacement therapy in Gaucher disease prevents progressive marrow infiltration when initiated before severe cytopenias develop; early diagnosis through newborn screening or family cascade testing is important.
Occupational and infection-control precautions in TB-endemic settings reduce exposure to Mycobacterium tuberculosis, the most common infectious cause of myelophthisis in high-burden countries.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid all contact sports and activities with significant fall or injury risk when platelet count is below 50×10⁹/L.
Inform all healthcare providers of your diagnosis and current blood counts before any invasive procedure — platelets must be checked and may need to be transfused before surgery or biopsies.
Maintain nutritional support — adequate protein and calories — even when anorexia is significant, since malnutrition further impairs residual haematopoiesis.
Seek immediate medical attention for fever above 38°C, and do not wait to see if it resolves, since neutropenia from myelophthisis makes infections dangerous within hours.

recommended: High-protein foods (eggs, fish, lean meat, legumes) to counter the catabolic state of advanced cancer or systemic inflammation impairing residual marrow function
Iron-containing foods or supplements only if iron deficiency is confirmed, since many myelophthisis patients are iron-replete from transfusions and adding iron risks toxicity
Small frequent meals to manage early satiety from organomegaly
Adequate hydration to maintain renal function, particularly in patients receiving bisphosphonates or nephrotoxic antimicrobials
avoid: Raw or undercooked foods when neutropenia is present, to reduce bacterial and fungal foodborne infection risk
Alcohol, which suppresses bone marrow function and interacts with cytotoxic chemotherapy
High-dose iron supplements without confirmed iron deficiency, since chronic transfusion frequently causes iron overload

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise should be adapted to haematological status. Gentle walking and light activities of daily living are safe at haemoglobin ≥8 g/dL and platelet count ≥30×10⁹/L. All strenuous exercise, contact sports, and activities with fall risk should be avoided when platelets are below 50×10⁹/L. Bed rest may be necessary during treatment of acute complications. Physiotherapy and occupational therapy improve functional capacity and reduce fatigue in patients with prolonged bone marrow failure.
prognosis: Prognosis in myelophthisis is determined primarily by the underlying cause and its responsiveness to treatment. Infectious myelophthisis — particularly from tuberculosis or histoplasmosis — has an excellent prognosis if the causative infection is diagnosed promptly and treated effectively; haematological recovery is typically complete within 2-4 months of effective antimicrobial therapy. Gaucher disease myelophthisis responds well to enzyme replacement therapy with sustained haematological improvement in the majority of patients on adequate treatment. Myelophthisis from haematological malignancy has variable prognosis depending on tumour type, stage, and treatment response. Metastatic solid tumour myelophthisis carries the worst overall prognosis, as extensive marrow involvement indicates disseminated stage IV disease; median survival is typically 3-12 months in this group, with outcomes dependent on anti-cancer therapy response. Severe cytopenias at presentation — haemoglobin <8 g/dL, platelets <50×10⁹/L, or ANC <0.5×10⁹/L — predict greater transfusion dependency and infection risk, which independently worsen outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Recovery timelines vary by aetiology. Infectious myelophthisis from TB typically shows haematological improvement within 4-8 weeks of effective treatment and full recovery within 3-6 months. Gaucher disease responds over 12-24 months of ERT. Haematological malignancy treatment responses — when achieved — improve marrow function within 6-12 weeks. Metastatic solid tumour marrow infiltration rarely achieves sustained haematological recovery without systemic disease control; transfusion requirements may escalate despite systemic treatment.
complications: Severe pancytopenia with life-threatening bleeding, sepsis, or profound anaemia — the direct consequences of failure to produce blood cells when marrow is replaced by more than 50-75% tumour or fibrosis.

Pathological fractures — vertebral compression fractures and long bone fractures through metastatic deposits are common in solid tumour myelophthisis and carry risk of spinal cord compression requiring emergency intervention.

Iron overload from chronic transfusion — each unit of packed red cells delivers approximately 200 mg iron with no physiological excretion mechanism; patients receiving more than 20 units over their lifetime develop hepatic, cardiac, and endocrine iron toxicity.

Tumour lysis syndrome — acute release of intracellular contents from rapid tumour cell death during anti-cancer treatment in the context of high marrow tumour burden can cause hyperkalaemia, hyperuricaemia, hypocalcaemia, and acute kidney injury.

Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — malignancy-associated DIC is particularly common with marrow-involving cancers such as acute promyelocytic leukaemia and metastatic mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma, causing simultaneous bleeding and thrombosis.

supportResources.name: National Cancer Institute — Bone Marrow Disorders
supportResources.description: NCI overview of bone marrow diseases, cancer metastasis to marrow, and haematological support for cancer patients.

supportResources.name: National Gaucher Foundation (USA)
supportResources.description: Patient and family resources for Gaucher disease including enzyme replacement therapy guidance, support groups, and research updates.

supportResources.name: NHS — Bone Marrow Failure (Aplastic Anaemia)
supportResources.description: NHS explanation of bone marrow failure causes including infiltrative causes, symptoms, and management, with information on specialist referral.

sources.title: Humphrey PA, Dehner LP, Pfeifer JD. The Washington Manual of Surgical Pathology — Bone Marrow Infiltration Patterns, 2021
Barosi G. Myelophthisic anaemia — classification and pathogenesis. Haematologica, 1993
Weinreb NJ et al. Effectiveness of enzyme replacement therapy in Gaucher disease: a 5-year prospective study. Arch Intern Med, 1995
Kyle RA, Rajkumar SV. Multiple myeloma — diagnosis and treatment. Mayo Clinic Proceedings, 2005
WHO — Treatment of tuberculosis: guidelines, 4th edition (WHO/HTM/TB/2009.420)
NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines — Hematopoietic Growth Factors v2.2025
American Society of Hematology — Aplastic Anaemia and Bone Marrow Failure Disorders

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary myelofibrosis (osteomyelofibrosis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary myelofibrosis is a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by JAK2/CALR/MPL mutations where fibrosis displaces marrow; myelophthisis is a broader syndrome of marrow replacement by any external infiltrating process. Both produce a leukoerythroblastic picture but have different treatments and prognoses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Aplastic anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aplastic anaemia causes bone marrow failure through hypocellular marrow (immune-mediated stem cell destruction), not infiltration; the marrow biopsy shows empty fat-filled space rather than infiltrating cells, and the blood film lacks the dacrocytes and leukoerythroblastic features of myelophthisis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myelodysplastic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MDS causes dysplastic but hypercellular or normocellular marrow with no infiltrating external material; cytopenias arise from ineffective haematopoiesis and apoptosis rather than physical displacement; dysplastic cell morphology on biopsy distinguishes it from myelophthisic infiltration.

confusedWithConditions.name: Haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: HLH causes cytopenias through hyperactivated macrophage phagocytosis of blood cells rather than physical marrow space occupation; bone marrow biopsy shows haemophagocytosis within macrophages, not tumour infiltration; ferritin is markedly elevated (often >10,000 µg/L) and the clinical context differs.

relatedConditions.name: Osteomyelofibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary myelofibrosis is one of the most common intrinsic causes of myelophthisis; both produce leukoerythroblastic blood pictures, splenomegaly, and marrow failure, making their clinical distinction essential.

relatedConditions.name: Aplastic anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both aplastic anaemia and myelophthisis cause marrow failure with pancytopenia and leukoerythroblastic features, but through opposing mechanisms — empty marrow versus infiltrated marrow — and are distinguished on bone marrow biopsy.

relatedConditions.name: Other bone marrow failure syndromes
relatedConditions.relevance: Myelophthisis belongs within the broader family of marrow failure syndromes; shared diagnostic evaluation starting with bone marrow biopsy and CBC links the management of all these conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Myelophthisis
relatedConditions.relevance: Self-referential placeholder; myelophthisis always occurs secondary to an infiltrating underlying disease that must be identified and treated.

faqs.question: What is myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: Myelophthisis is a form of bone marrow failure where normal blood-forming cells are displaced by infiltrating abnormal cells or material — such as cancer metastases, granulomas, fibrosis, or lipid-filled macrophages. The marrow loses the ability to produce adequate red cells, white cells, and platelets.

faqs.question: What causes myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: The most common causes in adults are metastatic cancer (especially breast and prostate cancer spreading to bone marrow), blood cancers such as myeloma and lymphoma, primary myelofibrosis, disseminated tuberculosis or fungal infections, and Gaucher disease. The marrow is physically replaced by the infiltrating material.

faqs.question: What is a leukoerythroblastic blood picture?
faqs.answer: A leukoerythroblastic picture means the blood film shows teardrop-shaped red cells, nucleated red blood cells, and immature white cells (myelocytes) circulating in the blood — findings that should not be present in healthy adults. This pattern indicates the bone marrow is being infiltrated and is releasing cells prematurely.

faqs.question: Is myelophthisis the same as aplastic anaemia?
faqs.answer: No. Aplastic anaemia causes marrow failure through immune destruction of stem cells, leaving an empty marrow. Myelophthisis causes failure because the marrow space is filled with invading cells or tissue. The bone marrow biopsy shows opposite findings: empty and hypocellular in aplastic anaemia, packed with abnormal material in myelophthisis.

faqs.question: What are teardrop cells (dacrocytes) and why do they appear?
faqs.answer: Dacrocytes are red blood cells deformed into a teardrop shape as they squeeze through marrow sinusoids packed with tumour or fibrous tissue. The deformation becomes permanent and is visible on the blood film. Their presence is a reliable indicator of marrow infiltration and is the key sign prompting a bone marrow biopsy.

faqs.question: Can myelophthisis be cured?
faqs.answer: Myelophthisis from a reversible cause — such as tuberculosis or histoplasmosis — fully resolves with effective antimicrobial treatment. Gaucher disease is effectively managed with enzyme replacement therapy. Cancer-related myelophthisis depends on the anti-cancer treatment response; it is rarely curable but can improve with disease control.

faqs.question: How is myelophthisis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with recognising the leukoerythroblastic blood film pattern. A bone marrow trephine biopsy is then performed to identify the infiltrating material type. Additional tests depend on the suspected cause — cancer staging scans, TB cultures, protein electrophoresis for myeloma, or enzyme assays for Gaucher disease.

faqs.question: What cancers cause myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: The most common solid tumours causing marrow infiltration and myelophthisis are breast cancer, prostate cancer, lung cancer, gastric cancer, renal cell carcinoma, and neuroblastoma in children. Among blood cancers, multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and leukaemia frequently infiltrate the bone marrow.

faqs.question: What symptoms does myelophthisis cause?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fatigue and breathlessness from anaemia, easy bruising and bleeding from low platelets, recurrent infections from dysfunctional white cells, bone pain from marrow infiltration, and symptoms of the underlying disease causing the infiltration — such as weight loss, fever, or night sweats.

faqs.question: How does Gaucher disease cause myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: Gaucher disease is an inherited enzyme deficiency in which glucocerebroside accumulates inside macrophages. These enlarged lipid-filled cells — called Gaucher cells — progressively fill the bone marrow, crowding out blood-forming cells and causing anaemia, low platelets, and splenomegaly. Enzyme replacement therapy clears the accumulation over time.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for myelophthisis from tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Disseminated tuberculosis causing myelophthisis is treated with standard anti-tuberculosis therapy — rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 6 months. Haematological improvement typically begins within 4 to 8 weeks of starting effective treatment, with full recovery in most patients within 3 to 6 months.

faqs.question: Is myelophthisis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. It causes bone marrow failure with resulting pancytopenia that directly increases the risk of life-threatening haemorrhage and severe infection. The prognosis depends on the underlying cause — infectious myelophthisis is often reversible, while cancer-related myelophthisis indicates advanced disease with a significantly worse outlook.

faqs.question: What blood test shows myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: The complete blood count reveals anaemia, often with low platelets and variable white cell count. The peripheral blood film examination is the most revealing test, showing the leukoerythroblastic pattern of teardrop red cells, nucleated red cells, and immature granulocytes — the combined finding that triggers bone marrow biopsy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myelophthisis and myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Primary myelofibrosis (osteomyelofibrosis) is a specific clonal blood cancer where scarring replaces marrow, and it is itself one cause of myelophthisis. Myelophthisis is a broader syndrome describing marrow replacement by any infiltrating material — cancer, infection, fibrosis, or storage cells. All myelofibrosis eventually produces myelophthisis, but not all myelophthisis is myelofibrosis.

faqs.question: Can transfusions help myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: Transfusions of packed red cells and platelets provide essential haematological support while the underlying cause is treated. They prevent immediate life-threatening anaemia and bleeding but do not treat the marrow infiltration. Patients who respond poorly to underlying disease treatment often become transfusion-dependent.

faqs.question: What is enzyme replacement therapy for Gaucher disease?
faqs.answer: Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) involves regular intravenous infusions of recombinant glucocerebrosidase — the enzyme deficient in Gaucher disease — every 2 weeks. It reduces lipid accumulation in marrow macrophages and over 12 to 24 months improves anaemia, platelet counts, and spleen size in the majority of patients.

faqs.question: Why does myelophthisis cause splenomegaly?
faqs.answer: When the bone marrow can no longer produce blood cells, the spleen and liver take over this function — a process called extramedullary haematopoiesis. The spleen enlarges as it ramps up blood cell production, causing abdominal fullness, left upper quadrant discomfort, and early satiety.

faqs.question: What is a bone marrow biopsy and does it hurt?
faqs.answer: A bone marrow biopsy is performed under local anaesthetic at the back of the hip bone (posterior iliac crest). A hollow needle removes a small cylindrical core of bone and marrow for laboratory analysis. Most patients feel pressure and a brief sharp pain during the aspiration pull; the whole procedure takes about 15 to 20 minutes.

faqs.question: When should cancer patients be tested for myelophthisis?
faqs.answer: Cancer patients should be evaluated for myelophthisis when they develop unexplained anaemia, low platelets, or a leukoerythroblastic pattern on a blood film — especially if they have bone pain or a known predilection for marrow metastasis such as breast or prostate cancer. A bone marrow biopsy is indicated when marrow involvement is suspected.

faqs.question: Does myelophthisis always cause pancytopenia?
faqs.answer: Not always in early stages. When infiltration is patchy and limited, the remaining haematopoietic tissue compensates and counts may stay near normal. Pancytopenia develops when more than approximately 50 to 75 percent of marrow haematopoietic space is replaced, or when the infiltrating process suppresses normal stem cells via cytokine mechanisms beyond simple space competition.

metaDescription: Myelophthisis is bone marrow failure from infiltration by cancer, infection, or fibrosis. Learn causes, leukoerythroblastic signs, diagnosis, and treatment.
